annotations.label: Schrödinger equation

anchor.snippet: The Schrödinger equation is a partial differential equation that governs the wave function of a non-relativistic quantum-mechanical system

annotations.note: Grep for 'Schrödinger' returns no results in Mathlib; this physics PDE is not formalized.

annotations.label: Position-space Schrödinger equation (1D)

anchor.snippet: position-space Schrödinger equation for a single nonrelativistic particle in one dimension

annotations.note: No Schrödinger equation (in any spatial form) appears in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: position-space Schrödinger equation for a single nonrelativistic particle in one dimension

annotations.label: Wave function

anchor.snippet: is a wave function, a function that assigns a complex number to each point

annotations.note: The quantum-mechanical wave function is not a defined object in Mathlib.

anchors.snippet: is a wave function, a function that assigns a complex number to each point

annotations.label: Quantum state as Hilbert space vector

anchor.snippet: defines the state of a quantum mechanical system to be a vector

annotations.note: Mathlib has (complete) inner product spaces, i.e. Hilbert spaces, but no notion of 'quantum state'.

annotations.label: Observable

anchor.snippet: are represented by observables

annotations.note: Self-adjoint/symmetric operators (the math behind observables) are formalized, but 'observable' as such is not.

annotations.label: Eigenstate

anchor.snippet: in which case it is called an eigenstate

annotations.note: An eigenstate is exactly an eigenvector of an operator, captured by Module.End.HasEigenvector.

annotations.label: Quantum superposition

anchor.snippet: known as a quantum superposition

annotations.note: Linear combinations exist abstractly, but no quantum-superposition notion is defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Born rule

anchor.snippet: with probability given by the Born rule

annotations.note: The Born rule is a physical postulate with no formalization in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Generalized eigenvectors

anchor.snippet: composed of elements outside the Hilbert space

annotations.note: Rigged-Hilbert-space generalized eigenvectors (continuous spectrum) are not formalized; Mathlib's algebraic generalized eigenvectors are a different concept.

anchors.snippet: composed of elements outside the Hilbert space

annotations.label: Time-dependent Schrödinger equation

anchor.snippet: the time-dependent Schrödinger equation, which gives a description of a system evolving with time

annotations.note: The time-dependent Schrödinger equation does not appear in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Probability density function

anchor.snippet: is taken to define a probability density function

annotations.note: Mathlib defines probability density functions generally via MeasureTheory.HasPDF/pdf.

anchors.snippet: is taken to define a probability density function

annotations.label: Stationary states

anchor.snippet: wave functions can form standing waves , called stationary states

annotations.note: Stationary states of the Schrödinger equation are not formalized.

annotations.label: Time-independent Schrödinger equation

anchor.snippet: Stationary states can also be described by a simpler form of the Schrödinger equation

annotations.note: It is the eigenvalue equation Hψ=Eψ, abstractly captured by HasEigenvector, but the Hamiltonian/equation itself is absent.

annotations.label: Wave function as eigenfunction of Hamiltonian

anchor.snippet: the wave function is an eigenfunction of the Hamiltonian operator

annotations.note: The eigenfunction/eigenvector relation is formalized abstractly, but not for any Hamiltonian operator.

annotations.label: Linearity of the Schrödinger equation

anchor.snippet: The Schrödinger equation is a linear differential equation

annotations.note: The Schrödinger equation is absent, so its linearity / solution-space superposition is not stated.

anchors.snippet: The Schrödinger equation is a linear differential equation

annotations.label: Unitarity of the time-evolution operator

anchor.snippet: is known as the time-evolution operator, and it is unitary

annotations.note: Mathlib proves the exponential of a self-adjoint element is unitary, but not Stone's theorem for the time-evolution group.

anchors.snippet: is known as the time-evolution operator, and it is unitary

anchors.value: {\displaystyle {\hat {U}}(\delta t)^{\dagger }{\hat {U}}(\delta t)\approx ({\hat {U}}(0)^{\dagger }+i{\hat {G}}^{\dagger }\delta t)({\hat {U}}(0)-i{\hat {G}}\delta t)=I+i\delta t({\hat {G}}^{\dagger }-{\hat {G}})+O(\delta t^{2}),}

annotations.label: Generator is Hermitian

anchor.snippet: To see that the generator is Hermitian

annotations.note: Hermitian/self-adjoint operators are formalized, but the generator-of-unitary-group claim (Stone's theorem) is not.

anchors.snippet: To see that the generator is Hermitian

anchors.value: {\displaystyle {\hat {U}}(\delta t)^{\dagger }{\hat {U}}(\delta t)\approx ({\hat {U}}(0)^{\dagger }+i{\hat {G}}^{\dagger }\delta t)({\hat {U}}(0)-i{\hat {G}}\delta t)=I+i\delta t({\hat {G}}^{\dagger }-{\hat {G}})+O(\delta t^{2}),}

annotations.label: Stationary solution

annotations.note: Stationary solutions of the Schrödinger equation are not defined in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Spatial part equation

anchor.snippet: The spatial part of the full wave function solves the equation

annotations.note: The spatial equation is the eigenvalue problem Hψ=Eψ, abstractly captured by HasEigenvector, but not for the Hamiltonian.

annotations.label: Energy eigenstates form a basis (spectral theorem)

anchor.snippet: The energy eigenstates form a basis

annotations.note: Mathlib has the finite-dimensional spectral theorem (orthonormal eigenbasis) but not the spectral theorem for unbounded self-adjoint operators.

annotations.label: Density matrix
